papers.title: Credibility-Weighted Pricing of Autonomous Vehicle Liability Under Operational Design Domain Shift
papers.abstract: Automated Driving System deployments create a foundational ratemaking challenge: sparse experience, shifting operational design domains, and non-stationary risk across software releases. We propose a hierarchical Bayesian credibility framework pooling across cities, software versions, and territories via a learned ODD-similarity kernel, nesting Buhlmann-Straub as a limiting case. Demonstrated on 648 verified-engaged Waymo crashes across four U.S. metros from the NHTSA Standing General Order database against 116 million matched miles, city-aggregate credibility weights are moderate (0.12-0.46), partial pooling decisively outperforms no pooling, and a power analysis shows the learned kernel's advantage becomes detectable at approximately twelve deployed cities.

papers.title: TerraTransfer: Learning End-to-End Driving Policies Without Expert Demonstrations
papers.abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving has achieved state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks and real-world deployments. Its standard training recipe, however, is expensive across all stages: collecting and labeling millions of driving frames is costly, and closed-loop RL on images is bottlenecked by the per-step cost of photorealistic rendering plus a forward pass through a large vision backbone. Self-play in vectorized simulators changes the economics: millions of rollout steps per second, and a state distribution naturally rich in collisions, near-misses, and recoveries that no driving log contains. Our approach exploits this asymmetry by decoupling learning to drive from learning to see. We pretrain a single policy by self-play, then align its latent space with a pretrained vision backbone, through the action KL divergence and a batch-relational low-rank structural loss. The action target comes from the self-play policy, so alignment never supervises against a logged trajectory: a paired dataset of (image, scene-state) frames suffices, with no need for the curated expert demonstrations that imitation pretraining is built on. On photorealistic 3D Gaussian splatting closed-loop scenarios, the resulting end-to-end policy matches or exceeds prior end-to-end methods.

papers.title: DriveJudge: Rethinking Autonomous Driving Evaluation with Vision-Language Models
papers.abstract: Autonomous driving has shifted towards end-to-end policy learning, where reliable, interpretable policy evaluation is a fundamental challenge as driving quality is highly context-dependent. Commonly used rule-based driving metrics like EPDMS are interpretable but lack context-awareness, while recent VLMbased evaluations are context-aware but limited by ambiguous VLM outputs and weak physical grounding. To evaluate driving in a manner that is both interpretable and context-aware, we introduce DriveJudge. DriveJudge is a driving evaluation agent that combines rule-grounded evaluation with Vision-Language Model (VLM) reasoning and selectively invokes physically-grounded deterministic rule functions after interpreting the environmental context. To train and evaluate DriveJudge, we curate a large-scale dataset of 33,577 challenging driving samples with human annotations on whether the driving behavior is reasonable in the given scenario. With this dataset, we address the underexplored problem of driving metric evaluation, and introduce two human-aligned benchmark tasks: Driving Quality Classification and Trajectory Preference Selection. DriveJudge outperforms EPDMS for driving quality classification by 21.23 AUC, and the recent VLM-based DriveCritic for trajectory preference selection by 6.5%, setting a new standard for interpretable and precise driving evaluation.

papers.title: Beyond Benchmarks: Continuous Edge Inference for Fine-Grained Roadside Perception
papers.abstract: Continuous AI inference on resource-constrained edge hardware introduces deployment effects that are largely invisible to conventional benchmark evaluation, including temporal instability in streaming video, thermal throttling under sustained load, and workload-dependent performance variability. We present Edge-TSR, a deployment-oriented continuous edge inference system for sustained roadside perception on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano. Edge-TSR integrates detection, tracking, fine-grained classification, and a lightweight track-aware temporal stabilization mechanism that improves streaming inference consistency with negligible computational overhead. Our central finding is that benchmark-centric evaluation systematically overstates deployed edge inference performance. Across three state-of-the-art baselines, we observe consistent 20-30% relative degradation when transitioning from static-image evaluation to real-world streaming deployment. Edge-TSR addresses this gap through temporal inference stabilization, recovering up to 10.16% classification accuracy over per-frame inference baselines while maintaining sustained real-time performance under continuous operation. We evaluate the complete system under diverse real-world deployment conditions, jointly characterizing inference quality, latency, throughput, and thermal behavior during long-duration operation. A 55-minute vehicular deployment over a 26 km route demonstrates sustained operation at 16.18 FPS within safe thermal limits on a single embedded device without cloud offload. Our findings show that deployment-aware evaluation and temporal inference stabilization are necessary components of continuously operating edge AI systems intended for real-world sensing deployments. We release a sample annotated streaming video evaluation dataset and full system implementation to support reproducible deployment-centric evaluation.

papers.title: Intermittent Strategic Cooperation of Two Selfish Agents on Graphs
papers.abstract: We study strategic space- and time-constrained cooperation between two self-interested agents through the Intermittent Strategic Cooperation-Based Two-Agent Path Planning (IC2PP) problem, a shortest-path game on graphs in which agents navigate toward individual targets while optionally cooperating at specific nodes to reduce their own travel times. Although such cooperation can strictly benefit both agents, it is strategically fragile: agents may deviate at any point along their paths. Modeled as a 2-player game, we characterize the structure of Pure Nash Equilibrium (PNE) joint strategies in IC2PP, and show that stable cooperation must follow a highly constrained form. We further prove that at least one PNE exists in every instance of IC2PP, and present a polynomial-time algorithm for enumerating all relevant PNEs. When multiple equilibria arise, we study coordination mechanisms based on bargaining-theoretic selection concepts and empirically compare equilibrium outcomes in terms of individual travel times and social welfare.

executive_summary: New foundation models and benchmarks (Qwen-RobotManip, EBench, GeneralVLA-2, EgoInfinity, AnnotateAnything) push large-scale, generalist robot learning, especially for manipulation.

Multiple works target more reliable, safer control: warm-started trajectory optimization for space manipulators, latency-aware diffusion policies, learnable priors, and uncertainty-aware VLA policies.
Navigation and mapping advance via scalable learned navigation backbones, visual scale-aware policies, radar-based SLAM, and active-perception planning for UAVs.

Whole-body and dexterous interaction see progress with a compact low-cost anthropomorphic hand, humanoid whole-body control benchmarking, multisensory manipulation, and rapid damage adaptation in soft systems.

Skill acquisition and continual learning are enabled by sleep-phase consolidation, inference-time visual verification, reinforcement learning over world–action models, and unified human–robot pretraining.
Assistive and social settings benefit from personalized exoskeleton adaptation, abstention-aware rearrangement, and socially informed pedestrian trajectory prediction and cooperative path planning.

institution_matches.title: Transformer-Based Warm-Starting for Feasible and Optimal Terminal Approach to Tumbling Objects with Space Manipulators
institution_matches.overview: Introduces a causal transformer warm-start for the computational bottleneck in sequential convex programming for space manipulator terminal guidance to tumbling targets. Decomposing translation and coupled attitude–manipulator planning, the method cuts second-stage SCP iterations by up to 28% and runtime by 23%, while preserving control cost and improving nonconvex feasibility projection robustness.

institution_matches.title: Continual Online Personalization of Exoskeleton Control via Manifold-Aware Experience Replay
institution_matches.overview: Proposes a manifold-aware experience replay framework to preserve user-specific exoskeleton control across varying gait tasks during online adaptation. By replaying experiences organized on a learned gait manifold without explicit task labels, it mitigates catastrophic forgetting and improves torque and gait phase tracking by 40–60% over non-replay baselines.

topic_matches.title: EBench: Elemental Diagnosis of Generalist Mobile Manipulation Policies
topic_matches.overview: Presents EBench, a simulation benchmark of 26 mobile manipulation tasks annotated along capability and generalization axes to go beyond single success-rate metrics. Evaluating leading generalist policies (π0, π0.5, XVLA, InternVLA-A1), it reveals divergent skill profiles and failure modes across dexterous and mobile subtasks despite similar overall success, enabling fine-grained diagnosis for model design.

topic_matches.title: Qwen-RobotNav Technical Report: A Scalable Navigation Model Designed for an Agentic Navigation System
topic_matches.overview: Introduces Qwen-RobotNav, a scalable base navigation model with a parameterized interface over task modes and observation configurations, enabling reconfigurable perception–planning at inference. Trained on 15.6M samples with vision–language co-training, it achieves state-of-the-art on multiple navigation benchmarks and supports hierarchical planners that dynamically switch modes for long-horizon tasks.

topic_matches.title: WireCraft: A Simulation Benchmark for Industrial DLO Manipulation
topic_matches.overview: Introduces WireCraft, a benchmark for industrial deformable linear object (wire/cable) manipulation across connector insertion, clip routing, and channel seating tasks with configurable difficulty and physics models. Comparative evaluations of RL, IL, and VLA policies show privileged-state RL can solve representative tasks, while vision-based policies still struggle with contact-rich alignment, highlighting open challenges in dexterous DLO manipulation.

topic_matches.title: EAGG: Embodiment-Aligned Grasp Generation via Geometry-Aware Graph Conditioning
topic_matches.overview: Proposes EAGG, a cross-end-effector grasp generator that encodes each hand or gripper as a topology-aware graph and low-dimensional control space, with a frozen backbone producing geometry-aware tokens. Iterative geometry injection keeps conditioning synchronized with evolving hand configurations, achieving near-specialized performance across six end-effectors and strong zero-shot transfer on the MultiGripperGrasp benchmark.

topic_matches.title: A Hybrid Optimization Framework for Grasp Synthesis under Partial Observations
topic_matches.overview: Combines an energy-based model with ICP in an SVGD optimization loop to synthesize robust grasps from partial point clouds. Across 5,360 attempts on 67 objects, the hybrid approach attains 60.9% success, outperforming AnyGrasp, GPD, and AS-ICP, demonstrating the value of coupling learned priors with geometric refinement for grasp planning.

topic_matches.title: LAGO Policy: Latency-Aware Asynchronous Diffusion Policies with Goal-Directed Collision-Free Planning for Smooth Manipulation
topic_matches.institution: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology; POSTECH; The University of Hong Kong
topic_matches.overview: Introduces LAGO, an asynchronous diffusion-based control framework that accounts for inference latency and integrates trajectory optimization for smooth, collision-free manipulation. It conditions diffusion on future actions for temporal consistency, predicts task-relevant interaction goals, and refines trajectories with spatio-temporal optimization, yielding smoother, safer executions in real-world experiments.

topic_matches.title: HumanoidArena: Benchmarking Egocentric Hierarchical Whole-body Learning
topic_matches.overview: Presents HumanoidArena, a simulation benchmark for hierarchical whole-body humanoid control where high-level policies map egocentric inputs to intermediate whole-body actions executed by motion trackers. Seven leg-critical HOI/HSI tasks expose tight coupling between decision-making and locomotion, showing that performance is highly tracker-dependent and cross-tracker transfer of intermediate actions remains fragile.

topic_matches.title: FLAP: FOV-Constrained Active Perception Planning for Prior-Map-Free 3D Navigation
topic_matches.overview: Proposes FLAP, a trajectory optimization framework that embeds field-of-view and sensing constraints directly in the planning problem for UAV navigation in unknown 3D clutter. Using sensor-frame perception constraints, a velocity-triggered activation mechanism, and an active perception sub-trajectory, it supports aggressive 3D maneuvers without prior maps, improving safety and efficiency in simulations and real flights.

topic_matches.title: RICH-SLAM: Radar SLAM with Incremental and Continuous Hilbert Mapping
topic_matches.institution: Chinese Academy of Sciences; Hong Kong University of Science and Technology; University of Science and Technology of China; Zhejiang University
topic_matches.overview: Introduces RICH-SLAM, a radar-based SLAM system combining a Rao–Blackwellized particle filter with an incremental Hilbert-space reduced-rank GP map. The method converts sparse, noisy radar returns into continuous, uncertainty-aware occupancy maps and uses posterior-aware particle weighting, enabling robust localization and planning in adverse conditions.

topic_matches.title: DexLink Hand: A Compact, Affordable, 16-DOF Linkage-Driven Hand with Human-Like Dexterity
topic_matches.institution: National University of Singapore; Zhejiang University
topic_matches.overview: Presents DexLink, a fully self-contained anthropomorphic hand with 20 joints driven by 16 actuators, fitting within human-hand size at under 400 USD. A hybrid planar–spatial linkage architecture delivers biomimetic thumb motion, joint synergies, and high load capacity, achieving maximum Kapandji score and all 33 Feix grasp types in experiments.
